plainMeaning: This verse blesses the Jain community by picturing it as a vast, calm ocean — ringed by the shore of steadfast resolve and teeming with the mighty 'sea-creatures' of devoted scriptural study.
recitation: bhaddaṁ dhii-velā-parigayassa, sajjhāya-joga-magarassa | akkhohassa bhagavao, saṅgha-samuddassa rundassa ||

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt bhadda < Skt bhadra 'good, auspicious, welfare'; nom./acc. sg. neuter used adverbially as a benedictory exclamation.
wordByWord.notes: Recurring refrain governing the genitive chain that follows: 'bhaddaṁ + genitive' = 'welfare to ___'.

wordByWord.etymology: dhii < Skt dhṛti 'firmness, fortitude, steadfast resolve' + velā < Skt velā 'shore, tide-line, coastal limit'; first member of the bahuvrīhi qualifying the ocean.
wordByWord.notes: Per the sutra's ocean-imagery, dhṛti is read as 'steadfastness', not dhī 'intellect'; velā is the encircling coastline/tide that bounds the sea.

wordByWord.gloss: of [the ocean] encompassed / surrounded (by that shore)
wordByWord.etymology: Pkt parigaya < Skt parigata (pari- 'around' + √gam 'go'), 'gone round, encircled, surrounded'; gen. sg. -assa agreeing with samuddassa.
wordByWord.notes: Completes the bahuvrīhi: 'whose shore-tide is steadfastness'.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt sajjhāya < Skt svādhyāya (sva- 'one's own' + adhyāya 'study/recitation').
wordByWord.notes: Preface glossary term; here forming a compound with joga.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt joga < Skt yoga (√yuj 'to yoke, join'), 'application, practice, union'.

wordByWord.gloss: of [the ocean] whose great sea-creatures (makaras) are ___
wordByWord.etymology: Pkt magara < Skt makara, 'crocodile, sea-monster, mighty aquatic creature'; gen. sg. -assa.
wordByWord.notes: Bahuvrīhi: 'whose makaras are the yoga of sacred study' — the formidable denizens that make this ocean unassailable.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt akkhoha < Skt akṣobha (a- 'not' + kṣobha 'agitation', √kṣubh 'to be stirred'), 'unstirred, imperturbable, fathomless'; gen. sg.
wordByWord.notes: Epithet of the ocean — never churned by storm; spiritually, untroubled equanimity.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt bhagavao < Skt bhagavataḥ, gen. sg. of bhagavat 'possessing bhaga (glory, fortune, majesty), worshipful'.
wordByWord.notes: Dignifies the Saṅgha-ocean as an object of reverence.
Preface glossary term; first member of the rūpaka-compound saṅgha-samudra.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt samudda < Skt samudra (sam- + ud- 'up-welling waters'), 'sea, ocean'; gen. sg. -assa — the head-noun of every genitive in the verse.
wordByWord.notes: saṅgha-samudda = 'the Saṅgha-as-ocean', the verse's governing simile.

wordByWord.etymology: Pkt runda 'wide, broad, vast' (= Skt viśāla; cf. rundra); gen. sg.
wordByWord.notes: Final epithet — the ocean's sheer expanse.

literalTranslation: Welfare to [the Saṅgha-ocean] encircled by the tidal-shore of steadfastness (dhṛti), whose great sea-creatures (makara) are the discipline of sacred study (svādhyāya-yoga) — to the unagitated, the venerable, the vast Saṅgha-ocean.
idiomaticTranslation: All auspiciousness to the Saṅgha — that vast, unshaken ocean! Its encircling tidal-shore is steadfast fortitude; its mighty sea-creatures are the discipline of sacred study; venerable and imperturbable, it stretches wide and deep.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: Where the previous verse blazed the Saṅgha as a sun outshining rival 'planets', this gāthā cools the vision into water: the Saṅgha is now an ocean, and every genitive in the verse hangs on samuddassa as a single sustained bahuvrīhi. Two mapped features carry the weight. First, dhii-velā — the 'shore-tide of steadfastness': just as the sea is held within its velā and never floods its bound, the community is contained and defined by dhṛti, the fortitude that keeps it within the discipline's limits. Second, sajjhāya-joga-magara — its makaras, the great sea-creatures, are not threats but the very practice of svādhyāya; an ocean is rendered formidable and inviolable by the leviathans within it, and so the Saṅgha's depth of study makes it unassailable. The two qualifying epithets sharpen the picture: akkhoha (akṣobha) — unchurned, imperturbable, no storm of passion or controversy stirs its depths; and runda — simply vast, immeasurable in extent. Crowning these is bhagavao, lending the seascape worshipful majesty. The opening bhaddaṁ keeps the benedictory current of the whole hymn flowing: this is not mere description but blessing — 'may welfare be to this ocean'. The verse thus pairs containment (the bounding shore of resolve) with depth (the study-creatures of the deep), so that breadth, calm, and disciplined power are praised together in one breath.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: The makara-image turns svādhyāya into the Saṅgha's defining strength. Sacred study is one of the six forms of internal austerity (abhyantara-tapa), and here it is the deep-dwelling power that makes the community inviolable — knowledge guarded and lived, not merely possessed. dhṛti (steadfast fortitude) functions as the shoreline that bounds conduct, echoing the equanimity at the heart of sāmāyika: the spiritual sea stays within its velā and is never flooded by impulse. akṣobha names that same imperturbability positively — the unstirred mind, free of the kaṣāyas (passions) that would churn it. Vastness (runda) and venerability (bhagavat) together affirm that a community grounded in study and resolve is at once boundless in reach and worthy of worship.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Recited within the fortnightly review, this verse asks the practitioner to test two things: the firmness of their own 'shore' — the resolve that keeps conduct within bounds — and the depth of their svādhyāya, the study that should run beneath daily life like creatures in the deep. Cultivate akṣobha: meet provocation and praise alike without letting the surface churn, drawing calm from depth rather than reacting on the surface.

crossReferences.text: Tattvārtha-sūtra 9.20 (svādhyāya as one of the six abhyantara-tapas)
Logassa (Caturviṁśati-stava): 'sāgaravaragambhīrā' — the venerated as deep as the foremost ocean

sources.title: Velā — Sanskrit/Prakrit/Pali lexica (shore, tide, coastal limit)
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of velā as 'shore, tide-line, limit' bounding the ocean (dhii-velā)

sources.title: Makara — Sanskrit/Prakrit lexica (crocodile, sea-monster, great aquatic creature)
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of magara/makara as the ocean's great sea-creature (sajjhāya-joga-magara)

sources.title: Akṣobha — Sanskrit/Marathi lexica (unagitated, imperturbable, fathomless)
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of akkhoha < akṣobha as 'unstirred, imperturbable'

sources.title: Samudra — Sanskrit/Prakrit/Jaina lexica (sea, ocean)
sources.consultedFor: Gloss of samudda < samudra as the governing head-noun 'ocean'

notes: The verse is one continuous bahuvrīhi: every -assa genitive (parigayassa, magarassa, akkhohassa, bhagavao, rundassa) qualifies saṅgha-samuddassa, governed by the benedictory bhaddaṁ ('welfare to ___'), matching the genitive benediction of the preceding gāthā. 'dhii' is read as dhṛti ('steadfastness') per the sutra's own ocean-imagery, not dhī ('intellect'); a minority reading taking it as dhī is possible and would yield 'shore-tide of wisdom'. 'runda' (= viśāla 'vast') is the rarer lexeme here and is glossed from its standard Prakrit sense; confirm against a critical edition where exact wording matters. Spelling (ધિઇવેલા, રુંદસ્સ, etc.) follows the Anchalgachchha/Vidhipaksha book verbatim, unnormalized.
